coredata.dc:title: Hierarchical flower-like CuO/carbon dots nanocomposite as advanced bifunctional electrocatalysts for clean hydrogen generation

coredata.dc:description: The rapid expansion of renewable energy relies on hydrogen, and it is the energy carrier with the highest energy density. Thus, effective hydrogen production is critical for successfully transitioning to renewable energy sources. So, identifying the effectual and efficient nanocomposite for water splitting remains the priority in context with sustainable hydrogen production. In the present work, we synthesized binder-free flower like crystalline shaped CuO nanoparticles with synthesized carbon dots using green method (CuO@CDs) catalyst loaded on glassy carbon (GC) electrode, which was characterized by XRD (X-ray diffraction analysis), SEM (Scanning electron microscopy), and EDX (energy dispersive X-rays spectroscopy) to analyze its physical and structural characteristics. The optimized sample, CuO@CDs/GC has a higher porous surface and smaller particle sizes as compared to the pure CuO/GC, and CDs/GC and it showed enhanced OER (oxygen evolution reaction) and HER (hydrogen evolution reaction) activity. Specifically, it had lower over potentials of 230 mV and 297 mV (V vs RHE) for both OER and HER at 10 mA cm−2, respectively. Moreover, the obtained Tafel slopes of 130 and 98 mV dec−1 for the nanocomposite are evidence of even more efficient OER and HER processes, respectively. Furthermore, Investigation of turnover frequency (TOF) was conducted and the findings indicated improvement of inherent activities (TOF = 0.006 s−1 and 0.00107 s−1 at 350 mV) for OER and HER and also attained double layer capacitance (Cdl = 9.5 mF cm−2). Hence, CuO@CDs/GC is a promising candidate for both processes under alkaline conditions due to its enhanced electrochemical activity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid expansion of renewable energy relies on hydrogen, and it is the energy carrier with the highest energy density. Thus, effective hydrogen production is critical for successfully transitioning to renewable energy sources. So, identifying the effectual and efficient nanocomposite for water splitting remains the priority in context with sustainable hydrogen production. In the present work, we synthesized binder-free flower like crystalline shaped CuO nanoparticles with synthesized carbon dots using green method (CuO@CDs) catalyst loaded on glassy carbon (GC) electrode, which was characterized by XRD (X-ray diffraction analysis), SEM (Scanning electron microscopy), and EDX (energy dispersive X-rays spectroscopy) to analyze its physical and structural characteristics. The optimized sample, CuO@CDs/GC has a higher porous surface and smaller particle sizes as compared to the pure CuO/GC, and CDs/GC and it showed enhanced OER (oxygen evolution reaction) and HER (hydrogen evolution reaction) activity. Specifically, it had lower over potentials of 230 mV and 297 mV (V vs RHE) for both OER and HER at 10 mA cm−2, respectively. Moreover, the obtained Tafel slopes of 130 and 98 mV dec−1 for the nanocomposite are evidence of even more efficient OER and HER processes, respectively. Furthermore, Investigation of turnover frequency (TOF) was conducted and the findings indicated improvement of inherent activities (TOF = 0.006 s−1 and 0.00107 s−1 at 350 mV) for OER and HER and also attained double layer capacitance (Cdl = 9.5 mF cm−2). Hence, CuO@CDs/GC is a promising candidate for both processes under alkaline conditions due to its enhanced electrochemical activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Our population is growing at a very fast rate this has led to increased demands for energy and the major energy sources are fossils [1]. The use of these fuels has resulted into emission of carbon dioxide and other pollutants that have worsened environmental issues [2]. For these problems to be resolved, the use of renewable energy sources must be incorporated, and one of these, hydrogen is particularly remarkable because it is virtually environmentally friendly, having high energy density, and are renewable [3]. Different methods of producing hydrogen have been highlighted that some of the most efficient ones is water splitting, even though it is non-spontaneous nature and high energy requirements. Thermal, photo, and electrocatalytic methods are the widely used categories of these techniques, and the best well known of these is electrochemical splitting of water for its effectiveness in terms of its cost [4]. The two primary processes in this process are the OER, and HER [5].

Nevertheless, the method of electrocatalytic water splitting face challenges arising from the high activation-energy nature of both the HER and the OER. Therefore, a significant overpotential is necessary to enable the desired level of current density for the process of water splitting [6], as given in equations 1–3.
(1)
Overallwaterspliting

However, thermodynamically water splitting requires 1.23 V, and water electrolysis uses 1.8–2.0 V owing to the uphill reaction mechanism [7]. Thus, the substantial overpotential and sluggish kinetics of the HER and OER make water splitting impractical. Hence, to minimize the overpotential and recover the efficiency of water splitting, extremely energetic electrocatalysts for the OER and HER are mandatory [8]. On the other hand, the benchmark electrocatalysts for OER and HER are oxides containing iridium and ruthenium as well as materials from the platinum group. However, these precious metals are too expensive and scarce for broad use [9]. On the basis of transition metals (TM), researchers are currently creating inexpensive electrocatalysts to address the issues of costly materials and scarcity in the field of OER and HER [10]. However, instances of compatibility degrading performances of water electrolysis are common. Compounds based on transition metals including phosphides [11], hydroxides [12], nitrides [13], carbides [14], and oxides [15], can indeed be employed for the bifunctional electrocatalytic water splitting, but their widespread use for sustainable hydrogen production is hindered by their inadequate conductivity and insufficient stability.

In the recent years, scientists have synthesized 3D transition-metal based catalysts that combine the characteristics of an HER and OER due to their ability to strongly bond to both hydrogen and oxygen-containing intermediates, thereby making it possible for them to function as bifunctional HER–OER catalysts [16]. Among the several types of transition-metal oxides like NiO [17], and CuOx based electrocatalysts are considered to be potential water spitting catalysts owing to characteristics like high stability, high electrochemical activity and good redox behavior [18]. For instance, Cui et al. have successfully fabricated CuO nanowires on copper foam by electrodeposition method and exhibited promising OER electrochemical activity of 430 mV overpotential and 44 mVdec-1 Tafel slope at 100 mA cm−2 [19]. Meanwhile, Yin et al. [20] designed Co-P/CuO nanowires grown on CF by electrodeposition method. The fabricated material exhibited better electrochemical activities showcasing lower overpotential (η) of 349 mV for OER, and 155 mV for HER at a current density of 200 mA cm−2. In addition, it exhibited Tafel slopes around 115 mVdec-1 and 79 mVdec-1 foe OER, and HER, respectively. Similarly electrocatalysts like Cu@CuxO [21], Fe/Cu(OH)2 − CuO [22], CoNiCu-LDH@CuO/CF [23] also produced exceptional catalytic properties in overall water electrolysis.

Furthermore, carbon dots (CDs) have received a lot of attention currently as electrocatalysts. The CDs are only about 10 nm or smaller in size and they have the competence of electrical conductivity [24]. Heteroatom doping, the process of adding additional atoms to these CDs, boosts the catalytic activity and adds more active sites. Contrarily, Sai et al. described notable research in which CuO@CQDs@CHNS electrocatalyst was prepared on GCE via one step hydrothermal method and it demonstrated encouraging action for OER and HER. For HER and OER, it measured an overpotential of 159 mV and 322 mV at 10 mA cm−2, respectively. In 1 M KOH, it obtained an onset potential of 1.83 V and 1.96 V vs RHE. But they still have high onset potential which needs further improvement, although selecting the correct catalyst chemicals and their combinations is important, the manufacturing process and the resulting shape also affect catalytic efficiency. Thus, the researchers have been worked on developing electrocatalysts which are binder free catalysts because, the presence of binder inhibit catalyst’s active sites, which lower the catalyst’s overall efficacy.

Inspired by all the key aspects that have been mentioned above, herein we have fabricated a novel CuO@CDs at the surface of GC electrode via candid hydrothermal technique for water splitting. In this case, the presence of CDs in the composite material enhances the surface area, active sites, and electrical conductivity. Additionally, they improve the overall catalytic efficiency by decreasing the charge recombination and increasing electron transit. This makes it a feasible option for electrochemical OER's long-term, effective hydrogen production. Furthermore, the combination mitigates issues like surface passivation by promoting stronger and longer-lasting catalytic activity under acidic or alkaline environments. Because CuO-carbon dots composites offer an economical and ecologically friendly way to produce clean hydrogen by electrochemical water splitting, they constitute a significant improvement in renewable energy technology and aid in the creation of sustainable energy systems. The fabricated electrocatalyst displays low overpotentials of 297 mV@10 mA cm−2 for HER and 230 mV@ 10 mA cm−2 for OER. The CuO@CDs/GC composite also exhibit a high durability of 100 h. In several energy-related applications, affordable, strongly linked, electrically conductive, and adjustable bifunctional OER/HER nanocomposites could replace noble metals like Pt or Pd.

Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), Potassium hydroxide (KOH), Urea (NH2CONH2,) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The solution for the whole research was prepared using deionized water (DIW). All of the analytical-grade chemicals and reagents utilized were unpurified.

The green method developed by Li Wang et al. was utilized to synthesis the carbon dots (CDs) used in this investigation [25]. In that process, 20 ml of water were mixed with milk (25 mL) and let it for a vigorous shaking for 15 min. After that, this mixture was heated for two hours at 180 °C in a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave. Ultimately, the solution was allowed to naturally cool to ambient temperature following the end of the predetermined reaction period. After allowing the mixture to reach room temperature, it was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm. After that, these precipitates were cleaned twice, first with ethanol and then with distilled water. The resultant precipitates were subsequently dehydrated for 18 h at 50 °C in a vacuum oven. We placed the brown CD precipitates in the desiccator once they had dried.

For the preparation of CuO nanoparticles, 0.1 M Cu (NO3)2 solution was prepared by dissolving in deionized water (60 mL) and shaked for 10 min to ensure complete dissolution. The solution was then constantly stirred while 0.1 M potassium hydroxide (KOH) was put in this solution to maintain pH (11–12) and enhance the precipitation, and after that, urea (0.005 g)was added to the mixture to attain the good morphology. The mixture was then stirred for two or more hours before being hydrothermally treated for 12 h at 160 °C within a stainless steel autoclave coated with Teflon. After the reaction, the contents of the autoclave were left to cool naturally to room temperature. The finished product was centrifuged to eliminate any remaining impurities and separate the CuO nanoparticles from the solution after being cleaned with distilled water and permitted to settle at room temperature for the whole night. Thus, the resultant CuO nanoparticles were dried, and then annealed at 450 °C for 5 h.

For the prepration of composite the as synthesized CuO nanoparticles (0.25 g) were added in 30 ml of deionized water as illustrated in scheme 1 . After that, the mixture was then added to 20 ml of water and milk (25 mL) and let it for a vigorous shaking for 15 min. After that, this mixture was heated for two hours at 180 °C in a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave. The remaining steps of the process were the same as outlined for the synthesis of CDs. The dried nanocomposite was kept for additional analysis and then applied for electrochemical characterization.

To fabricate the GCE (working electrode with 0.7 cm2 surface area), a slurry of CuO, CDs, and CuO@CDs nanocomposite was put together by mixing 10 mg in 1 mL of ethanol and then placed for ultrasonic bath for half hour to attain the homogeneous diffusion of nanoparticles at the room temperature. After that, the slurry of all the synthesized materials were subsequently drop casted on the GCE surface using micropipette. Then the electrode was left to dry at room temperature for whole night. Finally, the coated CuO, CDs, and CuO@CDs on GCE was used for further electrochemical investigation.

LSV (Linear sweep voltammetry),ECSA (electrochemical surface area analysis),CV (cyclic voltammetry), andEIS (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy) were the electrochemical techniques used in all of the OER and HER electrochemical experiments. An AUTOLAB potentiostat (PGSTAT-204) with digital control was used for the three-electrode arrangement system in which the experiments were conducted. After that the electrochemical cell was first washed with pure water several times, then with acetone, the electrochemical cell was then soaked in the mixture of 2 % H2SO4 and 5 %HNO3. Subsequently, the cell was removed and drained in oven at 70 °C for 35–40 min. Platinum wire (counter electrode) was cleaned with distilled water for some time then it was treated with 20 % HNO3 for a few minutes before placing it in the voltaic cell. Ag/AgCl, or silver-silver chloride, was used as the standard electrode, whereas CuO, CuO@CDs, bare glassy carbon (GC), and GC modified served as the working electrodes. These experiments were carried out with a 1 M electrolyte solution at 25 °C, containing just 1 M KOH at pH 13.59. For one hour, argon gas was used to purge the gas before the activity was ascertained, and to stabilize the activity of the electrode by 20 CV cycles were performed before attaining the results. It is possible to express the relationship between the EAg/AgCl (reversible reference electrode) and ERHE (reversible hydrogen electrode) as follows:

First of all, working electrodes were employed between 1. 0 and 2. 0 V RHE (at 50 mV/s) until a reproducible and stable signal of CV were achieved. LSV measurements of OER and HER were made where the scan rate was 10 mV s−1, and was used to calculate the Tafel slope, the overpotential and onset. The slope of Tafel was also calculated from the formula “η = a + b log j ”[26].

Thus, “b” is Tafel slope, “a” is a constant, and η represents the overpotential. Additionally, the following equation was employed to find the mass activity of each manufactured catalyst at a particular set overpotential [27].
(5)

The ECSA and Cdl were derived from the CV data collected in the non-faradic zone at various scan speeds (10 to 50 mVs−1). The slope values were acquired via performing a linear regression on the current density change (Δj = ja – jc) in relation to the scan rate. Half of the value of the Cdl is represented by the slope. Since a high Cdl denotes a high number of the electrode material's recognized catalytic active sites, it may be computed using equation (6).

In the equation, Csp stands for the specific capacitance of a glassy carbon electrodes (GC) with a value of 0.04 mF cm−2 for alkaline electrolytes [28]. Moreover, a key physical characteristic of an electrocatalyst is its turnover frequency (TOF), which quantifies its ability to initiate the electrochemical reaction required for the functionality of the materials. The formula (8) and (9) were used to determine the turnover frequency (TOF) value of the catalyst for HER and OER [28].

The above formulas display the OER and HER current density by j at a particular overpotential. Here, F (Faraday's constant), n (catalyst quantity), and A (glassy carbon electrode's geometric surface area). Utilizing Metrohm Autolab's computer-controlled potentiostat-204, the electrode/electrolyte interphase's solution resistance (Rs) and charge transmission resistance (Rct) were measured between 0.1 and 100 kHz.

The compositional state of the nanocomposite was investigated using Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1 (a), to verify the purity of the components. The synthesis was effective, and all of the product was distributed uniformly, as shown by the EDX spectrum. It also showed that the nanocomposite exclusively comprises C, Cu, and O atoms and is devoid of any other contaminants. Furthermore, the degree of crystallinity of the as-designed CuO, CDs, and CuO@CDs catalyst was assessed from the XRD patterns, as indicated in Fig. 1(b). The amorphous structure of CDs demonstrates a clear diffraction pattern that is very similar to previously published results, characteristic of amorphous carbon and a broad peak observed at 2θ = 20° correlates to diffraction angle (020). This XRD pattern aligning well with (JCPDS #00-049-1718). On the other hand, the XRD pattern for pure CuO were correspond to the peaks, which were fully matched with the JCPDS pattern (00-002-1040) suggesting crystalline nature of CuO. In contrast, the fabricated CuO@CDs reveals sharp peaks for CuO which suggest that it is crystalline in nature in the composite. The strong crisp peaks aligned with (002) and (111) hkl planes, while the peak observed, at 17° which is from CDs, confirming the phase purity and successful synthesis of the composite.

The N2 sorption isotherm was used to analyze the BET surface area and porous texture of CuO@CDs. According to BET results [Fig. 1(c)] show high specific surface area of (54.397 m2/g). These results suggest the presence of more active sites at the surface of CuO@CDs composite which may lead to highly exposed catalytic active sites on the surface of electrode.

Furthermore, Fig. 1(d) depicts a graph of particle size (nm) of CuO@CDs as measured by the Zeta particle size analyzer. From Fig. 1(d) the yellow peak from record 2 of CuO@CDs indicating their results which are mentioned in following Table 1 . From results, surface area is greater than average zeta particle size of CuO@CDs which increase the active sites and ultimately greater adsorption of OH– ions on the surface and higher activity of electrocatalyst, and it is also confirmed via BET surface area.

The SEM images given here show the morphological features of the CDs, and CuO@CDs composite at different magnifications which explain useful information on the microstructure and possible interaction between the two phases. Fig. 2 a and b represents the SEM images of the CDs, at low and high magnifications. The SEM shows the sheets like shape morphology of the CDs. In Fig. 2 (c), the surface morphology of the composite revealed the Carbon Dots (CDs) as round shaped particles surrounded by a rougher background. The average size of these CDs, which is approximately 8.50 nm, suggests a level of dispersion which could be crucial in improving the surface characteristics of the composite under use for catalysis, potentially in terms of the surface area which could be made available for further reactions. CDs are distributed randomly across the surface, which indicated a proper incorporation of these nanostructures by synthesis method. A closer view of the CDs is displayed in the Fig. 2(d) where the positioning and embedding of the CDs in the matrix can be well seen.

In Fig. 2(c), a larger scale of SEM image shows that there is the presence of both CuO particles and CDs where CuO has flower like crystalline shapes with average dimension of > 100 nm. The most important is the interaction between CuO and CDs; it can be observed that the CDs hindered the aggregation of CuO particles and keep them more separated and exposed. Fig. 2(d) further highlights the composite's microstructure, showing the CuO particles as irregular, yet well-defined crystals. The CDs, while smaller, are evenly distributed across the CuO surface, suggesting that they help to regulate the size of the particles and stop bigger agglomerates from forming. Because of the increased density of active sites and improved electron transport that this structural configuration allows, the composite's catalytic qualities should be improved. The elemental mapping images of carbon (e), oxygen (f), and copper (g) and (h) through (k) give substantial proof of the homogeneity of element distribution in the composite. The homogeneity of these elements reflects synthesis and incorporation of the CDs into the CuO network. Precise match of the composite in the overlay image, (k) ensures that the material is suitable for electrochemical application that do not favor much variation. Additionally figure I represents the creative representation of the surface-grown and embedded active material affects stability and comparable ion/electron mobility between the electrode and electron.

The electrocatalytic activities of CDs/GC, CuO/GC and CuO@CDs/GC catalysts were initially investigated for OER in alkaline condition. Consequently, in order to avoid the oxidation peak interfering with the start of OER, cyclic voltammetry (CV), and LSV curves that begin at elevated potentials and gradually decline are employed in this work to check OER activity after activating the electrode (20 CV cycles). First, a CV test was conducted to check the electrical response, stability of the CDs/GC, CuO/GC and CuO@CDs/GC as seen in Fig. 3 a and their corresponding LSV curves (inset Fig. 3a). As can be seen in Fig. 3(b), the CuO@CDs/GC electrode had the lowest overpotential (η) of all the manufactured electrodes used in this study, at 230 mV at 10 mA cm−2 calculated via CV curves (Fig. 3a). Aside from that, at 10 mA cm−2, the overpotentials of the bare GC, CDs/GC, and CuO/GC electrodes were 520, 490, and 420 mV, respectively. We mapped the η of each electrode at current densities of 50 and 100 mA cm−2 as displayed in Fig. 3(b) for more facile evaluation. The CuO@CDs/GC has consistently demonstrated a low η trend, achieving an impressive η of 468 mV at a high current density of 50 mA cm−2, which highlights its exceptional electrocatalytic capacity. The combined effects of CDs and CuO in CuO@CDs/GC enable the electrocatalyst surface to adsorb OER intermediates more effectively, thereby efficiency has been enhanced. The Tafel slopes, as illustrated in Fig. 3(c), were used to further examine the reaction kinetics of the as-fabricated electrodes as well as their capacity in producing current per applied potential. Among all CuO@CDs/GC has a lower Tafel slope (130 mV dec–1) than any other electrode, including bare GC (253 mV dec–1), CDs/GC (197 mV dec–1), and CuO/GC (158 mV dec–1). Thus CuO@CDs/GC catalyst exhibit improved OER activity in comparison to the monometallic CDs and CuO/GC electrocatalysts due to synergistic effect. Additionally, EIS was used to determine the charge transfer rate among the electrode and electrolyte. The CuO@CDs/GC nanocomposite exhibited a charge transfer resistance (Rct) of around 17.1 Ω, which suggests a notable decrease in resistance when compared to the individualmaterials. Fig. 3(d) results indicate that the charge transfer impedances of CuO/GC and CDs/GC are greater, with respective Rct values of 39.6 Ω and 74.4 Ω. During OER, our results showed that the CuO@CDs/GC nanocomposite facilitated quicker and more effective electron and proton transport at the electrode–electrolyte contact.

By comparing the mass activities of different catalysts, one can determine their superior catalytic activity which provides valuable information about the specific amount of mass needed to achieve maximum current density. An estimation of the mass activity was computed at an overpotential of 350 mV. Additionally, Fig. 3e presents a bar chart for comparison. The CuO@CDs/GC displayed the uppermost mass activity of 371 mA mg−1, indicating the presence of the greatest active sites for this catalyst. Meanwhile, the CDs and CuO/GC showed lower mass activities of 44 and 61 mA mg−1, respectively, resulting in lower OER performances [29].

The turnover frequency (TOF) is another crucial metric for comparing the intrinsic catalytic activity of catalysts prepared under varying conditions [30]. It is believed that all elements (Cu and O) sites on the catalyst surface participate in the OER for TOF calculations. The TOF of fabricated catalysts can be determined by using the mathematical relation (9). Fig. 3(f) illustrates a graphical depiction of TOF values obtained from all fabricated catalysts at different overpotentials. The turnover frequency (TOF) values for CuO@CDs/GC, CuO/GC and CDs were measured as 0.0061, 0.00088, and 0.000096 s−1, correspondingly at an η of 350 mV. The higher (TOF) of the CuO@CDs/GC electrode indicates its greater ability to transfer electrons, making it an exceptional catalyst with improved kinetics for OER. Enhancing performance in the OER process, which generates hydrogen, depends on the distinct roles played by the components in CuO/carbon dot composites. Copper (Cu), a crucial OER active site that improves electron transport and increases catalytic activity, is found in CuO. Numerous oxidation states of Cu+/Cu2+ aid in the formation of oxygen. Throughout the OER process, the oxygen (O) in the CuO lattice stabilizes the copper species and makes it easier for intermediates like *OH, *O, and *OOH to adsorb. Conversely, carbon dots (CDs) enhance the composite by boosting active sites, surface area, and electrical conductivity. They improve electron transit and minimize charge recombination, increasing total catalytic efficiency. Furthermore, CuO and CDs combine to form a composite material with superior charge transfer kinetics, larger current densities, and lower overpotentials. This makes it a potential option for electrochemical OER's long-term and efficient hydrogen production.

Equations presents the general electrochemical mechanism for the CuO@CDs/GC nanocomposite. According to the computation prediction, the oxygen generation cycle comprises four synchronized electron transfer procedures [31]. The OER commences with the adsorption of OH− at an active site (*) producing the *OH radical (Step 1). Subsequently, OH− deprotonates to generate O*, which comes with the elimination of an electron in the form of an electron and a water molecule (Step 2). Next, the hydroxide ion (OH−) attacks the double bonded oxygen, forming *OOH (Step 3). Lastly, a subsequent proton-coupled-electron transfer cycle leads to one more molecule of oxygen being formed along with the formation of one free active site (Step 4). (Ref 4).
* + OH− *OH + e− Step 1
*OH + OH− *O + H2O + e− Step 2
*O + OH− *OOH + e− Step 3
*OOH + OH− O2 + H2O + e− Step 4

Any step in the procedure could detain the overall OER performance [32]. This thorough mechanistic investigation clarifies the complex but well-structured series of chemical reactions that the CuO@CDs/GC nanocomposite experiences, identifying its catalytic potential in promoting effective OER.

The electrode’s stability was evaluated using chronoamperometry, which examined its long-term durability at a voltage (0.75 V) comparable with Ag/AgCl. The amperometry curves for the CuO@CDs/GC electrode, as presented in Fig. 3(g), showcased its durability throughout a 100-hour duration. After long-term stability test, the polarization curve of CuO@CDs/GC was again measured and compared with its original LSV curve (inset Fig. 3g) indicating the overlapped LSV’s trend showing exceptional durability even after 5000th CV cycles of OER performance. The findings revealed that the synthesized material exhibited exceptional stability and performance, particularly in the context of OER. Its remarkable long-term stability has established it as a prominent electrode material for OER applications. In Fig. 3(h) reaction mechanism of OER has been illustrated.

The HER experiment was performed using a three-electrode setup, similar to the one used for OER, to assess the dual functionality of the prepared catalysts. Fig. 4 (a) displays LSV polarization curves of various specimens. It is evident from the result above that CuO@CDs/GC sample which has been fabricated here shows the finest performance among the prepared samples, with a current density of 10 mA cm−2 resulting in an impressive η of 297 mV. At a current density of 10 mA cm−2, the CuO/GC and CDs electrodes showed greater overpotentials of 392 and 397 respectively, highlighting the advantageous effect of dual combination of CDs with CuO. The bar diagram in Fig. 4(b) shows how overpotential and current density relate to each other for the different samples. A linear increase with rising current indicates that more activation energy is needed to produce effective HER. Still, the CuO@CDs/GC electrode presented a relatively small overpotential value of 499 mV at 50 mA cm−2 which was higher than other electrodes fabricated on GC. The Tafel slope analysis, is presented in the Fig. 4(c), showcased electrocatalytic ability of the CuO@CDs/GC composite, at a Tafel slope of 98 mVdec-1. This suggests a much quicker electrochemical process when compared to CuO/GC and CDs, which showed Tafel slopes of 111 mVdec-1 and 117 mVdec-1, respectively.

Following that, on the surface of the electrode, an adsorbed proton remains by the decomposed water molecules in the alkaline medium, which start the HER.This step is identical to the Volmer step and has a Tafel slope of larger than 90 mVdec-1. In the Heyrovsky step, an electron generated at the electrode and a water molecule react with the adsorbed hydrogen atom. In the subsequent Heyrovsky step, an electron and water unite to produce a hydrogen molecule, which has a Tafel slope of 40 mV/decade. 30 mVdec-1 is the required Tafel slope value for the Tafel step, which is connected to the recombination of attached protons to the electrode surface.

In assessing the results, one may contend that the Volmer stage has a significant impact in determining the rate of each electrocatalyst that is being examined [33].
H2O + M + e− M−H* + OH− (Volmer step)
M−H + H2O + e− M + OH + H2 (Heyrovsky step)

Like OER, mass activities of fabricated electrodes have been evaluated in HER to check the catalytic activity by loaded mass. The CuO@CDs/GC displayed in Fig. 4e uppermost mass activity of 32.2 mA mg−1, indicating the presence of most active sites for this catalyst. Further, the CuO/GC and CDs/GC showed lower mass activities of 19.8 and 6.4 mA mg−1, respectively, resulting in lower HER performances.

TOF values were evaluated in Fig. 4(f) further to check the intrinsic catalytic activities for as artificial electrocatalysts in HER. As seen in Fig. 4f, CuO@CDs/GC exhibited (0.00107 s−1) two times greater TOF than remaining catalysts CuO/GC (0.0005 s−1) and CDs/GC (0.0000281 s−1) validating the greatest TOF of CuO@CDs/GC electrode.

Here, EIS was applied to analyze conductivity and electrochemical profiles of the specimens which are illustrated in Fig. 4(d). The CuO@CDs/GC electrode has a remarkable high charge transfer resistance (Rct) value of 337 Ω which is far lower compared to the Rct values of CuO/GC (486 Ω) and CDs/GC (1 kΩ). The decrease in Rct suggests that the addition of CuO@CDs/GC greatly improved the conductivity and interfacial electron transport of CDs, resulting in superior conduction besides efficient charge transmission abilities of the catalyst. As a result, the reaction kinetics were ultimately boosted.

Catalyst durability is critical in real-world applications. Long-term stability tests were carried out with a constant applied potential, as illustrated in Fig. 4(g). The CuO@CDs/GC catalyst displayed remarkable stability over 75 h, showing its potential as a good-performance electrocatalyst for practical applications. The stability test for both reactions showed no significant change, indicating that the produced electrocatalyst is highly durable and is capable of delivering robust performance.

The Electrochemical Surface Area (ECSA) is a dependable measure of the number of sites within the electrocatalyst that can be made available for electrochemical reactions. Improving the function of the electrocatalyst depends upon the density of active and effective sites which are proportional to the ECSA [34]. ECSA values were obtained from CV diagrams obtained at different scan rates (from 10 to 50 mVs−1), while working in the non-Faradaic zone of the CV curves as shown in Fig. 5 (a-c). The Cdl of the CDs/GC, CuO/GC, and the CuO@CDs/GC electrocatalysts were determined at 5.2, 7 and 9.5 mFcm−2, correspondingly, as depicted in Fig. 5(d). Through the application of the aforementioned equation to the results, it becomes clear that the ECSA values were obtained by dividing Cdl by Cs, which for flat electrodes is equivalent to 0.040 mF cm−2. ECSA values of 125, 175, and 237 cm−2 were computed for the CDs/GC, CuO/GC, and CuO@CDs/GC nanocomposite, respectively. The higher Cdl and ECSA values obtained in this CuO@CDs/GC nanocomposite indicate that such materials have higher concentration of active sites. During the water-splitting procedure, these locations are essential for the movement of protons and electrons at the junction between the electrode and electrolyte.

The OER, and HER performances of all the fabricated catalysts are provided in the Table 2 where again it is noted that among all the catalysts, CuO@CDs/GC catalyst is the most efficient. It is evident that the CuO@CDs/GC catalyst has excellent stability and efficiency even when compared to other comparable materials listed in Table 3 that have been reported in other recent studies.

The CuO/carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposites interact synergistically in OER and HER, improving electrocatalytic performance. In this case, CuO is a transition metal oxide with changing oxidation states, that has inherent catalytic activity which allows for effective charge transfer throughout the OER and HER processes. Additionally, CuO's catalytic effectiveness is limited by its small active surface area and low electrical conductivity. Thus, the inclusion of carbon dots, which have a wide range of functional groups, a large surface area, and good conductivity, overcomes these constraints. The CDs operate as a charge transfer bridge between the electrode surface and CuO's catalytic active sites, hence promoting the electron transport. Moreover, CDs enhance the dispersion of CuO particles, lowering agglomeration and exposing new reaction-active areas. By enhancing intermediate adsorption/desorption kinetics, the heterostructure created by the robust interfacial contact between CuO and CDs lowers the energy barriers for both OER and HER. Furthermore, the synergistic interaction between CuO and CDs influences the electronic structure of the composite, optimizing CuO's d-band core and enhancing its catalytic activity. Moreover, this hybridization ensures long-term performance during water splitting by strengthening the material's resilience in challenging electrochemical conditions. Consequently, the nanocomposites show strong bifunctional catalytic activity for the production of hydrogen and oxygen, which makes them attractive options for energy conversion applications.

The exceptional OER and HER activity of the optimized CuO@CDs/GC catalyst can be attributed to several factors: Also, (i) the Cu-CDs interaction, which improves the conductivity, electronic structure, and equilibrium binding energy of intermediates in the reaction and adsorption/desorption process; (ii) the uniform dispersion of the nanoparticles, which provides a large number of exposed active sites for charge transfer and the release of hydrogen and oxygen during the water splitting process, (iii) Without using any binder the CuO@CDs composite drop-casted on electrode, thereby maintaining the catalyst's original charge transfer efficiency and stability during prolonged OER and HER operations.

In summary, high efficacy binder free electrocatalyst have been prepared by hydrothermal method for highly efficient water splitting. Consequently, at 10 mA cm−2, the as-formed CuO@CDs/GC composite shown strong catalytic activity toward alkaline water splitting with a low overpotential of 230 mV for OER, a Tafel slope of 130 mV dec−1, and great stability for up to 100 h. These indices mean that the CuO@CDs/GC provides for the effective evolution of oxygen without energy losses, which evidences high catalytic activity and stability. For HER, the composite achieved stability for up to 75 h, a Tafel slope of 98 mV dec−1, and an overpotential of 297 mV at 10 mA cm−2 which indicates the ability of the composite to consistently evolve hydrogen for an extended period. Furthermore, the catalyst depicted superior mass activity, including an enhanced ECSA for both reactions, indicating a large number of active sites and improved electron transfer properties. These results depict that when CDs incorporated with CuO extends the catalytic properties by offering synergistic factors that facilitates electron transfer and surface reactivity. The overall higher observed activity in both OER and HER validates a strong possibility for CuO@CDs/GC as a highly efficient, long-lasting and cost-effective electrocatalytic material for hydrogen production from water.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Suleyman I Allakhverdiev is the Head of the Controlled Photobiosynthesis Laboratory at the K.A. Timiryazev Institute of Plant Physiology of the Russian Academy of Sciences (RAS). He obtained his BS and MS in Physics from the Department of Physics, Azerbaijan State University, Baku. He obtained his Dr. Sci. Degree in Plant Physiology and Biochemistry from the Institute of Plant Physiology, RAS (2002, Moscow). In 2018, he was recognized as the first in the ranking of the most highly cited scientists in Russia. In 2019, he was the winner of the K.A. Timiryazev Prize of the Russian Academy of Sciences. In 2021, he became the laureate of “Global Energy Prize” in the “non-conventional energy” category for his outstanding contribution to the development of alternative energy, particularly his design for a system of artificial photosynthesis and for his cycle of scientific works on bioenergy and hydrogen energy. In 2022, awarded a gold medal of Al-Farabi Kazakh National University “For Meritorious of Science”. In 2022, The Honored Scientist of the Russian Federation Title was given to him as an assessment of his outstanding contribution to plant biology development and the study of the fundamental principles of photosynthesis. In 2022, he was elected a corresponding member of the Russian Academy of Sciences. In 2018–2023, he was recognized by Thomson Reuters-WoS (Clarivate Analytics) as one of the Highly Cited world-class researchers selected for their exceptional research performance, and entered the list of the most highly cited scientists of the world (1% of the total number of researchers). He is in the World top 2% Scientist List (Stanford's List) (2020-2023). He has organized 11 international conferences on “Photosynthesis and Hydrogen Energy Research for Sustainability” (see: https://phrs-conference.com). The international academic platform for researchers: Research.com - International Leading Academic Research Portal published list of leading scientists for 2023 and 2024, and Suleyman Allakhverdiev was included in the ranking of leading scientists in the field of “Plant Science and Agronomy”, where he took 1st place among Russian researchers. It should also be noted that in 2022 he took 4th position in the ranking of the best scientists -biologists and biochemists in Russia (see: https://research.com). He has authored (or co-authored) more than 450 research papers in international Journals (Elsevier, Springer, Wiley) indexed in WoS, 42 book chapters, 7 patents and 13 books. His work has been cited above 30000 times, and his h-index - 85.

Sumaira Manzoor did her PhD in Physical Chemistry from Institute of Chemical Sciences, Bahauddin Zakariya University, Multan, Pakistan and is currently doing Postdoc at Interdisciplinary Research Center for Hydrogen Technologies and Carbon Management, King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals, Saudi Arabia under the supervision of Dr. Safyan Akram Khan. Her area of interest includes materials science, specifically: electrochemistry, photochemistry, biocompatible materials, and advanced energy storage/conversion materials. She is operating material characterization tools including XRD, FTIR, RAMAN, PL, UV-Vis, IV, SEM, EDX, and Electrochemical work station. Dr. Sumaira Manzoor is a leading expert in the fields of oxygen evolution reaction (OER), hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), overall water splitting, and water remediation, with a prolific publication record in international journals.

Dr. Safyan Akram Khan is a Research Scientist-I at the Interdisciplinary Research Center for Hydrogen Technologies and Carbon Management at King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals (KFUPM) in Dhahran, Saudi Arabia. Dr. Khan earned his M.Phil. degree from the University of Salford, UK, in 2000, followed by a Ph.D. in colloidal chemistry from the University of Bristol, UK, in 2004.

He has a strong academic and research background in colloidal chemistry, nanomaterials, and sustainable energy solutions. Over the years, his research has made significant contributions in the area of advance materials, hydrogen production and storage, CCUS, as well as energy storage technologies. With a distinguished research career, Dr. Khan has authored numerous high-impact publications and has played a pivotal role in the advancement of clean and sustainable energy solutions.

Beyond academia, Dr. Khan has extensive experience in coordinating, structuring, and leading industrial research projects for both local and multinational organizations. He has also served as the Program Manager for the flagship NextGenChem initiative at Lancaster University, UK, a program funded by the European Regional Development Fund, to establish collaborations with Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) to drive innovation in the chemical sector. Through his interdisciplinary research and leadership in industrial collaborations, Dr. Khan continues to contribute to transformative advancements in clean energy, environmental sustainability, and next-generation materials science.
